title: Goedhuis Waddesdon: ‘there is appetite for fine wine among the younger generation’
summary:
Goedhuis Waddesdon have supercharge their reach out to younger demographic over the last two years, arguing that “there is appetite for these things among the younger generation”.

The post Goedhuis Waddesdon: ‘there is appetite for fine wine among the younger generation’ appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Goedhuis Waddesdon have supercharge their reach out to younger demographic over the last two years, arguing that “there is appetite for these things among the younger generation”. According to Tilly Loughton, who has spearheaded the company's "firmer focus" with the under 35s on the private client sales team, younger consumers “want to taste good stuff, learn about good stuff, interact with it, and engage with it in a really nice way.” It must be admitted, her comments make a nice change from the oft-quoted doom and gloom narrative that the category is shrinking as younger consumers are not buying into it, but Loughton also notes that they are “a price conscious generation” and one that is buying wine in “a different way”. As Helen Miller, commercial director points out, en primeur is a challenge for this new generation "because it doesn't necessarily make sense to them in the same way that it would have done 50 years ago.” [That said, Goedhuis Waddesdon reported a mopre successful en primeur campaign than they "could have dreamt off", telling the drinks business there were some "stand out successes, and we weren’t necessarily expecting".] As Loughton continues, “en primeur used to be a system where it was so easy to buy and you'd know what you're getting, and definitely that it was at the best price, but given the market at the moment, there are compelling prices on the secondary market. So I do think that young people - and lots of lots of my older clients - navigate buying wine in a different way. They are not afraid to go for more mature vintages, not just the default youngest vintages every year and build their cellar that way. They're more open thinking about it.” As commercial director Helen Miller notes, this is of huge strategic importance. “We're really championing it because the reality is that our long term buyers already have a cellar full of wine and they're of an age where we need to be thinking about replacing them [the clients, not the wine]”, she explained. “It could be a Bordeaux tasting, and our customers will say very openly to me, ‘I won't be alive to drink that, so I'll buy the vintage that's drinking now’.”

New approach

But a different generation naturally requires a different approach, and with the nightmare that was Covid fading in the memory, face-to-face events are key, many with suppliers themselves. “It's so important to have face to face time with young people, actually getting them tasting wine and connecting with it,” Loughton explains. “Otherwise, it's a nebulous concept, on a list or something you can't really relate to.” As a result the team are doing more regular ticketed events and tastings with only “a little bit of an educational focus”. “Some are a bit more pitched at a wider audience, some might engage slightly more knowledgeable consumer, but we'll always assume that there'll be people in the room that don't actually know wine,” she explained.

Female focus

An important reason for doing ticketed events rather than just having it invitation only, and targeting only just existing clients, is that a lot of Goedhuis Waddesdon's younger private clients are men – and the team are making a conscious effort to attract a wider audience and a better male-female ratio. As db has previously reported, women are an emerging market for fine wine, and according to Queena Wong, founder of a female wine networking community Curious Vines, provide a massive opportunity for the merchant who can “get it right”. "In terms of a male-female split, there's still this belief that men actually become the buyers and the collectors,  but that said, at lots of our events, we do have a really nice human split of men and women, and there's definitely interest [from women]," Lougton said. "So that's why I think it's important to have these events open to all, because there's such a natural tendency to think 'who are my client, who would buy this?' and then you're narrow the potential pool." This also echoes the roughly 50/50 ration of the newly merged Goedhuis Waddesdon, which Miller explains has been a very positive move internally. “Goedhuis was very male dominated while Waddesdon was very female dominated, so having come together, we've got a critical balance,” she said. "I think we need to make a conscious effort to make sure that there are women at events and dinners."

title: Taylor’s launches first ever ‘Very Very Old White Port’
summary:
Taylor's has officially released its ‘Very, Very Old White Port’ which has been made using wines aged for more than 80 years in oak casks.

The post Taylor’s launches first ever ‘Very Very Old White Port’ appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content: Taylor's has officially released its ‘Very, Very Old White Port’ which has been made using wines aged for more than 80 years in oak casks. The limited-edition release, known as VVOP White, is blended from historic white ports, with some components dating back to 1896. The sweet wine will be available in limited quantities through selected fine wine merchants worldwide, with a recommended retail price of £580. The company said the wine is the result of generations of stewardship, with each generation of winemakers responsible for preserving the stocks before deciding whether they should eventually be bottled. “This is much more than launching a new Port wine. It is the culmination of generations of knowledge, patience and dedication. Every bottle reflects our commitment to preserving our heritage while continuing to innovate for future generations.”, said Taylor’s managing director Adrian Bridge.  He added: “Leading a company with more than three centuries of history is both an honour and a responsibility. We are custodians of an extraordinary legacy. Our role is to respect the past while ensuring the House remains relevant and inspiring for generations to come.”  Taylor's, which traces its history back more than 300 years, has never released an aged white port of this kind before. The launch comes as demand for rare and historic white ports has grown among collectors and fine wine enthusiasts. Head winemaker and master blender David Guimaraens said working with wines that had spent decades maturing in cask carried significant responsibility. "When you're working with Port wines that have been ageing for decades, you're making decisions that honour the work of people who are no longer here," he said. "Time has given this wine extraordinary depth while preserving remarkable elegance and freshness." The company said some of the wines in the blend originated from vineyards that no longer exist, having disappeared beneath the waters of the Douro following the construction of hydroelectric dams during the 20th Century. To accompany the release, the producer has commissioned a crystal decanter made in partnership with Portuguese luxury glassmaker Vista Alegre. The wine will be presented in a redesigned case using certified materials selected for their sustainability credentials.

title: Will Modelo and Corona brewing move from Mexico to the US?
summary:
US President Donald Trump is being urged by trade unionists to make some of America’s biggest beer brands “a lot more American”. But what is provoking the rift with Mexico over imported beer? db looks at the argument.

The post Will Modelo and Corona brewing move from Mexico to the US? appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
US President Donald Trump is being urged by trade unionists to make some of America’s biggest beer brands “a lot more American”. But what is provoking the rift with Mexico over imported beer? db looks at the argument. One of the country’s largest unions, The International Brotherhood of Teamsters, wants Trump to impose tariffs on Mexican beer and has reportedly stated that more of it should be brewed by US workers instead of being made across the border.

Advantages or disadvantages?

According to reports across the US, The International Brotherhood of Teamsters is now urging the administration to impose “significant tariffs” on Mexican beer and has made the request as the Office of the US Trade Representative (USTR) continues to assess whether certain countries’ policies have contributed to what are being levelled as “unfair manufacturing advantages”. Fox News also recently reported that Teamsters members have argued that Mexico has assisted its brewing industry through government-backed investment incentives, lower labour costs and export-focused policies. The union has purportedly also insisted this has expanded production and exports for Mexico and essentially helped imported beer capture a growing share of the US market. The suggested tariff push could, according to local reports, have knock-on implications for some of the US’s best-known imported beer brands such as the AB InBev-owned Modelo, Corona and Pacifico as well as the Heineken-owned brand Tecate all of which are currently brewed in Mexico before being shipped to US retailers. The International Brotherhood of Teamsters general president Sean O'Brien told Fox News: "We can brew Modelo beer. It's the same recipe. Let's brew it in the United States.” O’Brien insisted that the US should take over the brewing since “we are very good at producing goods and services in this country”.

Could beer production shift back to the US?

In its filing, the union flagged that Mexican beer production has increased 85% since 2014, with about 80% of the country's beer exports shipped to the US. Additionally, at the same time, capacity utilisation at major US breweries fell from 82% in 2013 to 65% in 2023, a trend the Teamsters lobbyists have said are now a threat to jobs. According to the union, tariffs would help shift more beer production back to the US, supporting US breweries, farmers and packaging businesses as well as those who oversee distribution. O’ Brien added: "I'm pro-America, pro-American worker, pro American jobs. So if there's an opportunity to tariff Mexican beer in this situation, which is going to provide opportunity for our current members, which we have tens of thousands in the brewery and distribution industry, then I'm all for tariffs and all for keeping American jobs in America.” The Teamsters told local news outlets that the threat to US brewery jobs is continuing to grow as major brewers still invest billions of dollars into Mexico.

Potential trade actions anticipated

According to a recent filing from the union, there are already plans for breweries to add another 19 million to 23 million hectolitres of production capacity over the next five years, an expansion it has said attributes to export-focused tax incentives, lower labour costs and industrial policies aimed at boosting manufacturing for foreign markets. The filing also is said to have cited Mexico's 2023 tax incentives for export-oriented manufacturers and its broader Plan México industrial strategy. The Teamsters' filing is one of several submitted as USTR weighs potential trade actions under its ongoing Section 301 investigation. The union has publicly backed tariffs as high as 75% on Mexican beer imports. The USTR has been contacted by db for full disclosure on how far-reaching the considerations are gathering pace in government as well as AB InBev and Heineken breweries with brewing arms in Mexico that currently import beer into the US.

title: India tightens rules on high-alcohol oral medicines to curb misuse
summary:
India has tightened regulations on oral medicines containing high levels of ethyl alcohol, requiring many of them to be sold only on prescription in a bid to deter misuse.

The post India tightens rules on high-alcohol oral medicines to curb misuse appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
India has tightened regulations on oral medicines containing high levels of ethyl alcohol, requiring many of them to be sold only on prescription in a bid to deter misuse. Under an amendment to the Drugs Rules, 1945, oral medicinal formulations containing more than 12% ethyl alcohol and sold in bottles larger than 30ml will now be classified under Schedule H1, a category of medicines subject to stricter controls. As part of the stricter rules, the tinctures will be sold only via prescription. Additionally, manufacturers will also now require a drug licence to sell them.  The change, announced through the Gazette of India, follows concerns that some alcohol-based medicinal preparations, including certain cough syrups, tonics and herbal tinctures, have been misused when sold over the counter.

Stricter measures

Schedule H1 was introduced in August 2013 through a government Gazette notification to impose stricter controls on medicines considered more prone to misuse than those covered under the existing Schedule H category. These include certain antibiotics, habit-forming medicines and drugs used to treat tuberculosis, according to the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare. Schedule H1 medicines cannot be freely purchased without a prescription. Pharmacies will be required to dispense the products only against a valid prescription from a registered medical practitioner, maintain a separate register of all sales and retain prescriptions and dispensing records for at least three years for regulatory inspection. The decision to add high-alcohol oral medicines to the list follows recommendations from the Drugs Consultative Committee (DCC) and the Drugs Technical Advisory Board (DTAB), which reviewed concerns over the abuse potential of alcohol-containing medicinal formulations. Officials said the decision was also influenced by complaints from several states, including Uttar Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh, where authorities alleged that some oral medicines were being consumed as substitutes for alcoholic drinks. By bringing these formulations under Schedule H1, the government hopes to limit inappropriate consumption while ensuring that patients with legitimate medical needs continue to have access under medical supervision.

title: Pioneering Malaysian brewery cleared of smuggling suspicions after police probe
summary:
1602, which professes to be the first craft beer brewery in Malaysia, has been cleared of wrongdoing following a police investigation into alleged smuggling offences. 

The post Pioneering Malaysian brewery cleared of smuggling suspicions after police probe appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
1602, which professes to be the first craft beer brewery in Malaysia, has been cleared of wrongdoing following a police investigation into alleged smuggling offences.  The probe was enacted on orders from Bukit Aman’s Wildlife Crime Bureau/Special Investigation Unit after a high-profile raid of the Sarawak brewery’s Kuching warehouse on 3 July. At the time, officials said the brewery was being examined under Section 135 of Malaysia’s Customs Act 1967, which covers offences including the possession or handling of uncustomed and prohibited goods, as well as customs-duty evasion. Officially founded in February 2023 by Malaysian brewer Randy Liew and German brewer Hans Gerner, 1602’s portfolio includes the products Pale Ale, Lager, Extra Dark and Wheat Beer.  The company, which alleges to be Malaysia’s first licensed craft beer brewery, has now been cleared of wrongdoing by the Attorney-General’s Chambers, with all seized products and merchandise returned to the company, according to 1602 managing director Datuk Seri Vincent Lee. 

'Professional, efficient and impartial'

In a statement, Lee thanked the Royal Malaysia Police for conducting a “professional, efficient and impartial investigation”.  “The findings ultimately confirmed that 1602 had not committed any smuggling offence, and all seized goods were therefore lawfully returned, enabling the company to continue its production, sales and business operations without interruption,” he added. Throughout the inquiry, 1602 had maintained that its beer was produced domestically in Kuching and not imported. Lee said the company supplied investigators with its brewery, wholesale and liquor sales licenses, as well as tax documentation in order to prove that the applicable duties and taxes had been paid. 

Smuggling probe

As quoted by Borneo Post at the time, he said: “Every 1602 product is legally brewed in Kuching and all applicable duties and taxes have been duly paid. “In these circumstances, the company cannot understand how products lawfully manufactured in Malaysia and fully tax-compliant could be treated as goods suspected of being smuggled.” Lee’s statement came after reports that officials had seized RM26.6 million (£4.83 million) in “contraband beer and heavy equipment” in what was described as one of Sarawak’s biggest smuggling busts, as reported by Sarawak Tribute. Yusri Hassan Basri, a senior Bukit Aman officer, said the raid uncovered “tens of thousands" of cartons of 1602 beer, on which duty was not paid.  Three Indonesian men, all aged around 35, were arrested during the operation.

title: Bordeaux vineyard values plunge as wine sales fall below three million hectolitres
summary:
Land prices across Bordeaux's leading appellations have continued to slide as the region's prolonged wine crisis deepens, with annual wine sales dropping below the symbolic threshold of three million hectolitres for the first time in modern history.

The post Bordeaux vineyard values plunge as wine sales fall below three million hectolitres appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Land prices across Bordeaux's leading appellations have continued to slide as the region's prolonged wine crisis deepens, with annual wine sales dropping below the symbolic threshold of three million hectolitres for the first time in modern history. Vineyard land values across Bordeaux fell further in 2025 as the region grappled with weak demand, shrinking vineyard area and another decline in wine sales. According to French regional newspaper Sud-Ouest, annual shipments from Bordeaux producers fell to 2.98 million hectolitres over the 12 months to the end of March 2026, down 12% year-on-year and beneath the three million hectolitre mark for the first time in decades. The figures underline the scale of the crisis facing France's largest wine-growing department, where growers continue to remove vineyards in an effort to rebalance supply with declining demand.

Prime appellations lose value

The downturn has affected almost every major Bordeaux appellation, although prestigious regions have proved more resilient than the wider market. The average price of vineyard land in Pauillac fell 22.7% between 2018 and 2025, from €2.2 million to €1.7m per hectare. Margaux declined 27.3%, from €1.1m to €800,000 per hectare, while Saint-Émilion dropped 25.9%, from €270,000 to €200,000. Elsewhere, the falls have been considerably steeper. Land values in Lalande-de-Pomerol have almost halved, falling 45.8% since 2018, while Fronsac declined 50%. Bordeaux Blanc vineyard prices dropped 51.5% and Bordeaux Rouge fell 60.6%. Some of the sharpest corrections have come in appellations already under economic pressure. Médoc vineyard land has fallen 81.8%, from €55,000 to €10,000 per hectare, while Saint-Émilion Satellites recorded a 73.7% decline, from €95,000 to €25,000. According to the data, Haut-Médoc values have dropped 62.5%, Blaye Côtes de Bordeaux 60%, Graves Rouge 37.5% and Sauternes 33.3%.

Vineyard area shrinks

The collapse in land values comes as Bordeaux undergoes one of the largest vineyard restructuring programmes in its history. Between 2023 and 2026, almost 30,000 hectares of vines will have been uprooted across the region. Around two-thirds of that area was removed through government-backed compensation schemes, with growers receiving either €6,000 or €4,000 per hectare depending on the programme. The remaining vineyards were removed without financial support. Despite the reduction, Gironde remains France's largest wine-growing department, with 91,264 hectares of vineyard in 2025, ahead of Hérault (72,901ha) and Aude (53,986ha). Many producers are concentrating production on their strongest vineyard sites rather than maintaining larger but less profitable holdings. Xavier Buffo, director of Château de la Rivière in Fronsac, told Sud-Ouest that the estate had removed around 20 hectares as part of efforts to focus on its best terroirs, while simultaneously acquiring vineyards in stronger locations. A proposed Foncière d'avenir ("Future Land Fund") is expected to help struggling growers complete land sales and support the redevelopment of former vineyard sites for alternative agricultural uses.

Climate compounds economic pressure

The latest figures arrive as Bordeaux producers contend with a succession of climatic challenges alongside weakening consumer demand. As previously reported by the drinks business, French vineyards have endured repeated heatwaves, drought, frost and hail in recent years, with June temperatures reaching as high as 44°C in parts of the country. Viticulture specialists have warned that prolonged heat is reducing yields, damaging fruit quality and increasing production costs at a time when many growers are already under severe financial strain.

title: Côtes du Rhône and Alsace join forces in Asia
summary:
In a joint presentation, Côtes du Rhône and Alsace set aside the old French script of heritage and hierarchy to talk about uniting themes of freshness, generational handover and wines that no longer need a dinner table.

The post Côtes du Rhône and Alsace join forces in Asia appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
In a joint presentation, Côtes du Rhône and Alsace set aside the old French script of heritage and hierarchy to talk about uniting themes of freshness, generational handover and wines that no longer need a dinner table. The white-wine-dominant Alsace joined forces with the red-wine focused region of Rhône in a series of presentations across Asia, to audiences of importers and trade in Japan, Hong Kong, Taiwan and Singapore. “We complement each other,” said Thierry Fritsch, head of training and conferences, at CIVA, the Alsace Wine Board. “They’re red, we’re white. We have the same philosophy of grape varieties; we don’t show off.” Julie Coutton of Inter Rhône, opening her half of the masterclass, agreed the fit is perfect. Being “unpretentious, simple and open-minded,” she said, “is truly something that we share.” Underneath the stylistic contrast, the two regions shared similar stories of celebrating diversity over ages, a generational handover in the cellar, a decisive turn towards freshness and wine stepping out of its formal, table and pairing-bound dining format.

Alsace: complex, not complicated

Alsace is long and narrow. The vineyards run more than 100 kilometres and by law, since 1959, its wines must go into the region’s distinctive slender flute bottle. “Maybe the shape of this bottle reminds us of the shape of the vineyards: very long, very narrow,” Fritsch mused. “And in all the wines you will taste a backbone of acidity. All our wines are built around this freshness.” The region boasts a high concentration of Michelin Guide restaurants, and the wines – which range from dry Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Blanc, among others – are produced to be enjoyed with food. He is candid about the complexity of the region’s baroque classification: seven main grapes and 53 appellations, of which 51 are Grand Crus sitting atop a single regional appellation. The wines and the region, he insists, are “not complicated – they’re complex.” Notably, roughly 75% of its wines are sold in France. “If you draw a circle with a 400-kilometre radius around Colmar, you will capture more than 80% of our sales,” he said. In export markets, Asia is about widening that circle, finding new markets and about perception. Having worked in Asian markets since 1997, Fritsch shared his deep understanding of how the wines find their places in Asian capitals. Japan was first to embrace Alsace, and over 30 years, he has witnessed the region develop a nuanced wine culture of its own. In Singapore, the top imported Alsace grape is not Riesling but Gewurztraminer. “I was surprised when I checked the figures,” he admitted, noting that “we don’t drink a lot of Gewurztraminer at home.” The other Singapore surprise is fizz. Crémant d’Alsace, which celebrates its 50th anniversary this year, has grown from 14% of the region’s production in 1997 to 40% today, and its sales in Singapore are climbing alongside the grand crus. He saw consumer trends across markets moving Alsace’s way: “People are looking for white, people are looking for more elegance in the red, and people are looking for bubbles.” In Shanghai, he has watched white wine climb from an afterthought to achieve parity and a then majority share in Cantonese-influenced Shenzhen. “The white wines are booming over there. It’s just amazing.” On food pairing, a reflex claim of every region courting Asia, three decades of travel has made him sceptical of formulas. “A lot of regions say ‘we produce food-friendly wines’. When you ask them how they match, they don’t know the answer,” he shared. The French instinct pairs spicy food with Gewurztraminer, whose “fatness will overcoat the spices and slow them down.” Asian palates, he has come to understand, often want the opposite, and Alsace’s dry Riesling fits the bill “because its backbone acidity, the Riesling will boost and reveal all the flavours of the dishes.”

Climate change

In the vineyard, Alsace’s response to climate change is not an abstract concept. “Strasbourg today has the climate Lyon had 50 years ago,” he said. “Growers are responding structurally, planting high-density vineyards up to 8–9,000 vines per hectare against a 4,500 minimum required in the grand crus, to push roots, temper ripeness and hold alcohol down.” Sustainability, meanwhile, is coded into the Alsatian way. Alsace’s first organic estate dates back to 1961, and today roughly 35% of the region’s surface is organic against a French average of 22%. “It’s not marketing. We produce wine in respect of the environment, the land and our consumers.” The clearest sign of change may be Pinot Noir, Alsace’s only red, now making up 13% of the production. “The Pinot Noir you drink now didn’t exist 30 years ago,” Fritsch said. “It’s not an evolution, but a revolution. The new generation has travelled a lot. They were in New Zealand, Burgundy, America and just learned how to produce Pinot Noir.” Beyond racy, elegant and precise Pinot Noirs, the young are experimenting with maceration and natural styles, drier wines, even trialling plantings of Assyrtiko. The older generation is handing over to the new. The prevailing attitude of fathers to sons and increasingly daughters, citing estates such as Domaine Pfister under Melanie Pfister, is one of generous handover. “This is your land, your heritage, and you have to continue as you see best,” is how Fritsch summed up the running sentiment. The new generation still respects the tradition, terroir and Alsace image. The distinction is the mindset: “Before it was fixed, it was square. Now it’s open.”

Côtes du Rhône recalibrated: 2000 years of adaptation

Roughly 500 kilometers south-east, Coutton’s argument starts from history, rather than image. “You cannot live and thrive and maintain your vineyards and keep making wine for 2000 years without adapting,” she said at the outset, as she introduced Côtes du Rhône’s deep-rooted tradition of winemaking and its constant evolution through the ages. The Romans made wine with added water, honey, salt, spices and fruit, a style she has tasted. “It’s extremely interesting, but it doesn’t taste good,” she admitted. Côtes du Rhône, nearly 90 years old as an appellation, was from the very outset written about diversity and blending, with 19 varieties codified in its founding rules in 1937. “Blending is part of our DNA,” said Coutton. “All our winemakers, at heart, they’re master blenders.” Today’s Côtes du Rhône winemakers work with a generous toolkit of 27 permitted varieties led by Grenache, Syrah, Mourvèdre. The wider Rhône Valley, whose vineyards stretch 200 km along the Rhône River from Lyon to Avignon, encompasses 32 appellations. Within it, the Côtes du Rhône family forms a dynamic quality pyramid of base-level Côtes du Rhône, Côtes du Rhône Villages without geographical name, Côtes du Rhône Villages and its 21 named villages and 18 crus at the summit. In 2025, production of these appellations was still dominated by red wine: around 85% reds, 8% whites and 7% rosés, with whites steadily growing in importance. Coutton wanted her audience to know the pyramid is not carved in stone. Cairanne joined the cru family a decade ago, Laudun was promoted to become the 18th cru in September 2024, Gigondas has had its appellation extended to white wines from the 2023 vintage; and the red-only named villages are now collectively pushing to have their whites recognised too. What drives a village up the ladder is people, not paperwork. "It's not just about getting quality recognised — it's not just a big file," she said. "It's because there's a collective of growers and winemakers working together to say: hey, what we do is important and deserves recognition."

Adaptation moving forward

The adaptation Coutton speaks of bends towards freshness. “It’s been mitigating the effects of climate change in the vineyard and cellars and finding strategies to work around that to retain freshness and balance,” she said. In practice, it means adapting to unusual conditions of very warm vintages on the one hand, and frosts such as in 2021 on the other. It also means using lesser-known varieties like Cinsault, Counoise, and Piquepoul to drive freshness in the blends.

A generational transition

Similar to Alsace, the generational change is defining. “There’s been a general move towards freshness, with the help of the younger generation coming into the family business,” said Coutton, estimating that 70–80% of Rhône companies now have 20–40-year-olds taking part in decision-making. This cohort is younger, educated, shaped by vintages in Australia, New Zealand, South Africa and the US and by shared years at the Lycée Viticole in Orange. Their parents are listening. Ask a grower why the estate has gone organic, biodynamic or regenerative, and Coutton finds the answer is often: “Because my kids wanted to do that and it makes sense. So, we’re doing it.” The sustainability move is deliberate and measured. “It's not bold moves, but small, simple, discrete ways to reduce our use of water, or to reduce waste that we generate,” she shared. “Right now, they've been trying and testing. In the next five to 10 years, we're going to see way more of that.” In the Rhône Valley, for the 2025 vintage, 25% of the harvest was certified as organic or biodynamic and 37% as HVE3. More than 60 per cent of total production is therefore covered by an environmental certification or label. And, echoing Fritsch on breaking the food pairing codes, Coutton also finds wine has moved away from the traditions of formal dining and pairings. “Wine does not only belong to the table. A big shift that’s happening in consumption is people drinking in all sorts of settings,” she said. This summer, Côtes du Rhône is rolling container wine bars into major French music festivals, like Musilac in Aix-les-Bains, headlined by Katy Perry this year to a potential crowd of 200,000. “There’s no big masterclass. We are there because the wines are very good and you can enjoy them anywhere, outside of the table, in a place where you’re just having a fun time.” In response to shifting tastes, Côtes du Rhône new campaign, ‘Refresh your wines’, targets 21–40-year-olds, inviting them to rediscover the appellation through a more contemporary approach that reflects today’s lifestyles and drinking occasions. “We are not shy to show bottles of reds that have been chilled. We can go so far as to put some ice cubes in a rosé – it’s part of the simple and not-taking-ourselves-so-seriously trend as well.” Find out more through the appellations' official channels:

CIVA (Comité Interprofessionnel des Vins d’Alsace)

https://www.vinsalsace.com/ IG: WinesOfAlsace | FB: AlsaceWinesOfficial

Inter Rhône (Interprofession des Vins AOC Côtes du Rhône et Vallée du Rhône)

https://www.vins-rhone.com/en   www.cotesdurhone.com

IG: cotesdurhone | FB: CôtesduRhône

title: Why it’s hoped Taylor’s latest innovation will go unnoticed
summary:
As db is first to report, Taylor's Port has developed a new, lighter bottle for its fine fortified wines – and we find out why it's hoped that consumers won't notice.

The post Why it’s hoped Taylor’s latest innovation will go unnoticed appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
As db is first to report, Taylor's Port has developed a new, lighter bottle for its fine fortified wines. We find out why CEO Adrian Bridge is hoping that consumers won't notice.

From 21 July, Taylor's will begin rolling out a redesigned bottle that weighs just 440g, down from 580g – a 24% cut in glass weight for one of the most recognisable shapes in the Port category.

Across the brand's annual production of more than 2.5 million bottles, the change is expected to save 350 tonnes of glass and 210 tonnes of CO2 a year, while shaving 84kg off the weight of every pallet shipped.

Work on the new bottle began in January 2024, and getting there took longer than Bridge had hoped. "We were trying to target 420g," he admitted during a discussion with db on Friday 17 July. "We stopped at 440g after a lot of transit and bottling line tests to make sure that the integrity of the bottle was there,” he added.

The main obstacle was the debossed lettering on the bottle's shoulder, a feature Bridge was not willing to sacrifice even though dropping it could have taken the bottle under 400g. "The debossing point is always a point of weakness," he said. "Had we eliminated the debossing element, then in fact we would have been able to go even lighter... but we find that that is an important part of the Taylor bottle: it's an iconic part of the packaging that was created back in the early 90s and has become well loved by consumers around the world."

That heritage matters more today than it did when the shape was launched, Bridge pointed out, given how much the brand's footprint has grown. "Back in the early 90s, when we launched this shape, we were probably only trading with about 40 markets in the world; today we trade in over 100."

Rather than trumpet the change, Bridge is deliberately playing it down. "I'm not sure that going out there claiming to be the lightest bottle is what this is about," he said. "If all companies get to a lightweight bottle, we'll be making more of a difference. I'm not really interested in are we the lightest, are we the oldest, are we the most creative... The point is what are we actually doing, and what we are doing here is dropping 24% of the glass weight."

For Bridge, the guiding principle is evolution rather than revolution – and keeping the shift as close to invisible as possible. Holding the old and new bottles side by side, he said the difference is barely perceptible. "For most consumers on a shelf, they won't notice the difference. They might notice the reshaping of the label more than they'll notice the actual bottle itself – it's a little bit shorter, but seen in isolation, you wouldn't notice that."

He argued that's often the point with genuine sustainability progress. "Perhaps some of the best environmental changes brands can make are where the end consumer doesn't necessarily notice the important steps you're taking,” he said – even though the move will help scope three measurements for Taylor’s customers by reducing carbon emissions.

Beyond the glass

The lightweighting push extends beyond the bottle itself. Taylor's has also switched its stopper from a PET-based synthetic cork to a fully biodegradable, fibre-based alternative. "It's a type of plastic – it’s a PEF (polyethylene furanoate), not a PET (polyethylene terephthalate)," Bridge explained. "So it's actually made from fibre and therefore is fully degradable."

Asked whether a wooden top might have been simpler, he told db: "Wood itself is not necessarily the answer... just making products that involve cutting down trees isn't necessarily better." The group has taken a similar approach elsewhere, switching its 50-year-old Tawny presentation box from oak to bamboo. "Bamboo is one of the fastest growing wood fibre products," he said, calling it "lighter and visually extremely attractive."

The new Taylor's bottle retains its punt and has relocated its spotting device from the base to the side, a change Bridge admitted was a technical detail few consumers would care about, but one that allowed the base to be made thinner.

"It's the sort of technical stuff that most consumers wouldn't even think about," he said, referring to this shift, which sees the mold cavity identification mark move from its previous location on the bottom of the bottle. "But these are the little things that you do that can help you to produce a bottle that is a lighter weight."

On glass colour and furnace efficiency, Bridge noted that Taylor's use of recycled ‘Euro-green’ glass allows furnaces to run at 100-110°C cooler than those firing virgin white flint glass.

He estimated that the new bottle, produced by Spanish manufacturer Vidrala at a plant in Portugal’s Marinha Grande, is around 85% recycled content.

Rolling it out

Taylor's has already applied the same lightweighting process to sister brand Croft, and to a gift decanter produced for Marks & Spencer. Bridge said the approach would be extended further where possible and urged the wider industry – and larger-volume producers in particular – to follow suit. "You've got to go to the bigger moving lines," he said. "That makes absolute sense... that's the lower hanging fruit that's going to make more of a difference."

Bridge also pointed to progress on renewable energy at the group's Portuguese operations, including two newly installed photovoltaic plants that bring the total to six, and a local energy-sharing arrangement with schools in Vila Nova de Gaia that will help push renewable output from 3,250 to more than 4,100 megawatt hours by 2032.

"This isn't rocket science," Bridge said. "But when brands – and particularly a brand like us – are doing it, it maybe encourages other people to go back and look at it too."

Read more below

The big interview: Adrian Bridge

Cockburn’s unveils lighter Port bottle

Or watch via The Drinks Business Instagram here.

title: Estonians brace for alcohol retail price spike
summary:
Estonia’s new alcohol licence fees are due to affect drinks retailing. But will store owners avoid the cost or simply pass it onto customers?

The post Estonians brace for alcohol retail price spike appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Estonia’s new alcohol licence fees are due to affect drinks retailing. But will store owners avoid the cost or simply pass it onto customers? Retailers across Estonia are facing the introduction of a new fee for alcohol licences that are set to reduce the number of outlets selling beer, wine and spirits. At present, around 10,000 alcohol sales licences have been issued across Estonia with the number having increased in recent years. In fact, according to the Eesti Rahvusringhääling (ERR), 90% of Estonian’s can reach a store that sells alcohol within 10 minutes of their homes, reflecting the expansion of the country’s off-trade which has been catering for high demand and positioning itself for local convenience. Despite these wins for the sector, the expansion has captured the attention of the World Health Organisation (WHO) which has now recommended using paid sales licences to reduce the number of sales outlets - a move that the Ministry of Social Affairs is now considering.

The knock-on effect

The fees are anticipated to have the knock on effect on the sector, reducing alcohol availability on such a broad scale while also assisting to push up prices for consumers as retailers pass on the costs or leverage the attraction of their drinks stock availability. Examples of the impact of the fees were outlined by the ERR, which highlighted instances where Leesi village shop in Juminda, which is a cooperative founded by local residents, is known for selling everyday items. According to the ERR, Leesi’s current alcohol sales account for up to 30% of sales, but if the new alcohol licence laws are passed, the store managers will need to pay the fee and if the price of drinks rises, the concern is that people may begin to seek out lower prices via illegitimate means. Leesi board member Ants Viirmaa said that although little will change if the fee is "symbolic" if it is not, it could have an “impact” and there could also be a danger of how people come by their alcohol. Viirmaa said: "Every tax is always ultimately reflected in the final price. If it becomes very high, it will certainly have an impact. It will be added to the price of the bottle, and the danger here is that if the price of the bottle becomes too high, then alcohol will simply, figuratively speaking, move from the shop to behind the shop," he said.

Squeezing out the independent retail sector

Rikets Flowers CEO Getriin Hermlin who sells alcohol as a side business alongside floristry, explained that for a flower shop chain, alcohol is an insignificant side business and is only available as a matter of giftability since customers may want to add a bottle of wine to a bouquet ordered from the online shop. But, if the alcohol sales licence becomes commercially unprofitable, the outlet would rather not be penalised for the convenience and instead would simply stop selling alcohol. Hermlin said: "It depends on what is decided, for what amount, and how much bureaucracy comes with it. If it is very complicated and expensive, then there is no point in my doing it.” Although Hermlin identified that this wouldn’t seem like it might affect the business much, it “will certainly” impact people’s decisions on where they buy their other items. She explained that there will always be “certain customers who choose somewhere else” and added that “whether it is Selver, where they can pick up that bouquet of tulips and a bottle of Champagne at the same time” Hermlin pointed out that what the WHO does not understand is that it impacts independent businesses and “the power then goes into the hands of the large retail chains”.

Stricter conditions will need to be met to obtain licences

The plan is for an analysis to be completed on what kind of sales licence system would be suitable for Estonia by the end of the year. In fact, the Ministry of Social Affairs suggested that the licence fees might tackle the proliferation of alcohol sales and also assist in authortiees being able to oversee the situation in a more controlled way. Speaking about the reasoning behind the fees, Ministry of Social Affairs advisor Kristiin Mikko added: “There is a system where, if an establishment wants to open a shop in an area where there are already shops selling alcohol, then, for example, it is more expensive. That is because the density is already so high, and there are systems where certain conditions have to be met in order to obtain one at all.”

title: Château Léognan unveils its new consultant winemaker
summary:
One of the (many) delights of writing for the drinks business as its Bordeaux correspondent is the opportunity to visit a property whose wines one knows by reputation only. Every now and again this throws up a jewel - and Chateau Léognan, under new consultant winemaker Sébastien Vergne, is one such jewel, writes Colin Hay.

The post Château Léognan unveils its new consultant winemaker appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
One of the (many) delights of writing for the drinks business as its Bordeaux correspondent is the opportunity to visit for the first time a property whose wines one knows only a little or by reputation only. Every now and again this throws up a jewel - and Château Léognan, under new consultant winemaker Sébastien Vergne, is such a jewel, writes Colin Hay. And so it is here, with the still as yet rather confidential Château Léognan the latest of my charmed discoveries. This is a property that I have visited now twice in the last three months. The most recent occasion was in May for an extensive vertical tasting arranged to introduce to me its new – and, as it turns out, rather talented – consultant winemaker, Sébastien Vergne. Léognan and Vergne are, I suspect, names that it would be good to remember. Situated in the extreme south-western corner of the appellation, the vineyard is very well-placed, close to Domaine de Chevalier and Châteaux de France, de Fieuzal and Le Sartre. It was planted in the 1980s by none other than Olivier Bernard as fermage for Domaine de Chevalier with the vines cultivated by the property under licence to, and exclusively for, its rather illustrious neighbour. This explains its relative anonymity. For until its purchase in 2007 by Philippe and Chantal Miecaze, no wine was bottled under the Chateau Léognan label. The vineyard remains today essentially as it was constructed by Olivier Bernard, with only the Petit Verdot planted more recently (and used for the first time in the 2025 vintage). It is small, just 6 hectares on sandy gravel in a single block entirely surrounded by forest. And that forest is extensive, with only 10% of the estate’s 60 hectares under vine. This of course gives impressive natural biodiversity, with the forest also locking in a certain freshness in the vineyard that it protects. No pesticides or chemical fertilisers have been used since the vineyard was first planted. All of this renders Chateau Léognan rather distinctive. But although that is certainly part of the secret to the quality and character of its wines, rather more significant still is the almost unprecedentedly high proportion of Cabernet Sauvignon in the vineyard and the final blend. This turns out to be a massive advantage in the period of seemingly accelerating climatic change through which we are living. The only downside is that the vineyard itself is also rather sensitive to frost (with notably diminished yields in 2013, 2016 and 2017). Since January 2025 the property has been working with Sébastien Vergne (formerly of Château Margaux) as its consultant winemaker. But as my tasting notes below attest, this is less an entirely new departure than the continuation of a long-standing record of excellent viticulture at the property. This is hardly surprising when one considers that between 2007 and 2012 it was none other than Marielle Cazaux who, alongside Philippe Miecaze, was the winemaker at Chateau Léognan before she left for Pomerol, first at Petit-Village and then at La Conseillante. The estate’s 6 hectares under vine are dedicated to Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot and (now) just a little Petit Verdot. A white (not tasted as part of this vertical) is produced under the château label with grapes sourced from the Graves. The wines are characterised by a naturally low pH, giving them a characteristic freshness that, in combination with the high proportion of Cabernet in the blend, helps so much in hot and dry vintages such as 2015, 2018, 2022 and 2025. Yet my tasting notes reveal a very impressive consistency in the wines regardless of the prevailing vintage conditions. Château Lêognan displays a rare capacity to deal with both wet and cold, on the one hand, and hot and dry on the other, with excellent results in 2018 and 2025 but also 2013, 2017 and 2021. The high percentage of Cabernet Sauvignon, the age of the vines and the sheer quality of the terroir are all significant factors. A typical blend for the property contains 70% Cabernet Sauvignon, a figure really only ever attained – and even then, only rarely – at Smith Haut-Lafitte and Domaine de Chevalier (where the Cabernet Sauvignon is typically around 60%). Carmes Haut-Brion has around 70% Cabernet in the vineyard, but the majority of that is, of course, Cabernet Franc. The property richly rewards a visit – and not just for the quality of its wines. Its delightful château buildings include a rare privately consecrated 18th century chapel. Both have been fully and sensitively restored. The Château itself now serves as a luxury hotel – with a stylish restaurant alongside. The grounds are extensive with the gardens designed by the Frères Buhler, more famous for Bordeaux’s beautiful Parc Bordelais.

Tasting notes

  • Particular highlights: 2009, 2011, 2012, 2015, 2019, 2020, 2025
  • Vintage revelations: 2013, 2017, 2018, 2021, 2024
Chateau Léognan 2007 (Pessac-Léognan; 70% Cabernet Sauvignon; 30% Merlot; a final yield of 19 hl/ha; pH 3.48; 12.8% alcohol). This is very attractive aromatically and impressively youthful, like really all of these wines. Nutty. Sweet-scented. Pure. Lifted. Aerial. And with pleasing cedary notes. Plums and dark berries. A touch of espresso. There’s no great length here, but this has aged gracefully and there is no hint of dryness. Drink relatively soon but this will hold for a good 5 years still. 91+. Chateau Léognan 2008 (Pessac-Léognan; 74% Cabernet Sauvignon; 26% Merlot; a final yield of 37 hl/ha; pH 3.66; 13% alcohol). This is a little more closed at first and takes a while to gather itself and reveal its charms. There’s a touch of horsehair. Charcuterie, too. The fruit is a little darker. Plums. Maybe a cherry or too. It’s decidedly more ample in frame and more substantial too. And its juicy and quite succulent at the core. There’s a slight residual sweetness from the oak. What I really like is the grip of the tannins and the hint of a fantail that forms on the rolling finish. 92. Chateau Léognan 2009 (Pessac-Léognan; 75% Cabernet Sauvignon; 25% Merlot; a final yield of 38 hl/ha; pH 3.53; 13.1% alcohol). This is altogether more classical and one senses, for the first time in the series, a little the heat and sunshine of the vintage. Currants. Red and darker berry fruits. But with fresher wild herbal notes too. A little liquorice. This is ample in frame and succulent at the core, pushing into the cheeks. It’s also impressively aerial and quite sinuous with little eddies and swirls in the space afforded the fruit by the ample frame. The best and the freshest of these in the series to date and very much at the top of the palate on the finish. 94. Chateau Léognan 2010 (Pessac-Léognan; 68% Cabernet Sauvignon; 32% Merlot; a final yield of 37 hl/ha; pH 3.55; 13.2% alcohol). This is excellent, even in the context of the vintage. But it is far from being my favourite in the flight. Tasted blind, I’d be impressive but in the context of this vertical I find it a little stolid and closed. It’s going through a phase, and it needs time! Horsehair. Game. Dark berry and stone fruits. This is ample, once again, and very dense and compact with less charm than the 2009 at least for now. This will go the distance but will never lose some of its slightly foursquare elements I suspect. Full, deep and layered. Long and gently rolling on the finish. 92. Chateau Léognan 2011 (Pessac-Léognan; 63% Cabernet Sauvignon; 37% Merlot; a final yield of 32 hl/ha; pH 3.61; 13% alcohol). In contrast to the 2010, this is open, juicy, aerial and quite lifted. We find garrigue herbs, a little liquorice, notes of charcuterie and game and a little oak smoke. Plum and baked bramble. There’s impressive depth and density to the mid-palate. I find this a little foursquare too, but it’s not as ample in frame as either the 2009 or 2010 and that rather helps. What we do have is more clarity and precision, impressively so. The 2011 is fresh on the finish and youthful, as you’d expect. It’s just starting to enter its drinking window and is ready to charm those open to having their image of this vintage recalibrated. 93. Chateau Léognan 2012 (Pessac-Léognan; 74% Cabernet Sauvignon; 26% Merlot; a final yield of 33 hl/ha; pH 3.65; 13% alcohol). There lots of Cabernet in the blend here and it’s used to lovely effect. Lifted and aerial, with almost a note of heather alongside the wild thyme, the dark berry fruits, the espresso coffee bean and tobacco pipe smoke. Ample, plump, plush, succulent and soft in contrast to more substantial and stolid 2010 and 2011! It is also beautifully sapid and juicy on the well-sustained finish. The last vintage made by Marielle Cazaux and it’s very much in her style. A coup de coeur. 93+. Chateau Léognan 2013 (Pessac-Léognan; 78% Cabernet Sauvignon; 22% Merlot; a final yield of just 10.2 hl/ha after the frost on the 6th of April; pH 3.70; 12.7% alcohol). This is bright and crisp in its red berry fruit freshness. The oak is imperceptible which is a very good thing in a vintage like this. For this is all about the purity of the fruit. It’s simple, easy, but very well managed. It’s soft, supple and engaging too, the tannins having none of the dryness that made this such a tricky vintage to taste en primeur (well, there’s maybe just a hint on the finish – but right at the end). It is also longer on the finish than you might if a little slender. Difficult to pick blind as 2013, which is a considerable compliment. 90. Chateau Léognan 2014 (Pessac-Léognan; 69% Cabernet Sauvignon; 31% Merlot; a final yield of 38 hl/ha; pH 3.58; 13% alcohol). After a series of rather singular wines from singular vintages, this is rather more classical in its composition. It’s gracious too and one of most accessible and charming wines of the tasting. It’s fresh and lifted like the best of these. It’s plump, plush and definitively juicy. The mid-palate is packed with dark berries and a little plum. Yet is also has the amplitude to push into the cheeks. There’s a touch of mocha on the finish and a little cedar. A lovely very balanced expression of the vintage. 93. Chateau Léognan 2015 (Pessac-Léognan; 61% Cabernet Sauvignon; 39% Merlot; a final yield of 32 hl/ha; pH 3.55; 13.4% alcohol). Lovely too. This is similar in a way to the 2014, definitely cut from the same cloth. But it’s a little richer, a little fuller and a little sunnier in its character and disposition, as you’d expect, with the accent in the fruit profile on confit strawberries! It’s ample, too, with perhaps a little more clarity in the layered mid-palate. The tannins are very fine and seem to work themselves between the layers rendering this both joyous yet serious at the same time. It captures the vintage very well – opulent, on the one hand, but accessible too. The ferrous minerality is very present on the juicy finish. 93+. Chateau Léognan 2016 (Pessac-Léognan; 70% Cabernet Sauvignon; 30% Merlot; a final yield of just 12.6 hl/ha after significant frost on the 3rd of May; pH 3.60; 13.2% alcohol). This is very different and more serious in a way. Fennel. Wild herbs. Crisp, dark berry fruits. A little charcuterie and that touch of ferrous minerality again. There’s even a little hint of tomato stem. This is saline in its minerality and almost a little Italian – I’m thinking Sangiovese from Léognan! It’s also very refined and almost luminous in its mid-palate clarity from the quality of the tannins. Juicy and quite sinuous despite the evident density, intensity and concentration of the fruit. In short, this is very impressive. 95. Chateau Léognan 2017 (Pessac-Léognan; 66% Cabernet Sauvignon; 34% Merlot; a final yield of just 7.4 hl/ha after the frost on the 27 and 28 April; pH 3.55; 12.8% alcohol). Lovely with plenty of very dark briary fruits, all crisp, crunchy and al dente. Soft, relaxed, expressive and rather hauntingly beautiful aromatically. Wild thyme, rosemary, cedar and those soft plump dark berries – blueberries and mulberries and a little loganberry. Pure, fresh and simple in a way but youthful, delicate and decidedly juicy on the finish. 93. Chateau Léognan 2018 (Pessac-Léognan; 71% Cabernet Sauvignon; 29% Merlot; a final yield of 39 hl/ha; pH 3.68; 13.1% alcohol). Aromatically there is little hint of the excessive heat and drought of the late summer. Indeed, this is cool at the core, balanced and harmonious – a tricky pick tasted blind. It’s ample and rich, of course, with nutty almost buttery notes on the mid-palate that are certainly a clue to the vintage. Cashmere in texture, sapid and fresh despite the richness and density. We may miss a few of the signature fresh herbal notes, but this is a very sapid and succulent wine. A great success and a tribute to the quality of the terroir. 94. Chateau Léognan 2019 (Pessac-Léognan; 70% Cabernet Sauvignon; 30% Merlot; a final yield of 34 hl/ha; pH 3.71; 13.2% alcohol). There’s lovely freshness here and plenty of aromatic lift. Dark plump berries, cedar and a little tomato stem once again, that lovely ferrous note underscoring the saline minerality. It’s succulent too in the dense and layered mid-palate. I love the cedar, graphite and fresh roasted coffee bean notes released with aeration in the mouth that generously enrobe the plump dark berries and damsons. There’s quite a lot of tannin here still to resolve, but this is lovely already with plenty of up-side potential. 95. Chateau Léognan 2020 (Pessac-Léognan; 67% Cabernet Sauvignon; 33% Merlot; a final yield of 41 hl/ha; pH 3.58; 13.1% alcohol). If anything, this is fresher still and it seems to have more of the character of the vintage than the 2019. We find cedar and graphite notes intermingling with the dark berries and that lovely damson skin freshness. There are wild herbs too. This is vivid and vibrant, despite the impressive density, with a lovely clarity and brilliance to the mid-palate. It’s perhaps my favourite of the entire series, with its long, bold and rippling finish. 95+. Chateau Léognan 2021 (Pessac-Léognan; 80% Cabernet Sauvignon; 20% Merlot; a final yield of 38 hl/ha; pH 3.58; 12.8% alcohol). At this stage, the highest even proportion of Cabernet Sauvignon in the blend and, of course, that is the key to the identity of a wine that transcends the vintage. Bright and crisp, the mouth filled with red berry fruits. A little like the 2017 this is very fresh and crunchy and impressively youthful too, having lost none of its pure-fruited initial charm. Cedar and mulberry – the fruit much darker on the palate. Lovely dark berries fill the slender but densely packed frame. Simple but with great purity and freshness. A wine to recalibrate expectations of a rather underappreciated vintage. 94. Chateau Léognan 2022 (Pessac-Léognan; 75% Cabernet Sauvignon; 25% Merlot; a final yield of 35 hl/ha; pH 3.60; 13% alcohol). Substantial. Colossal, even. This is a bit of a monster after the more subtle 2020 and 2021. Indeed, I could almost be tasting an en primeur sample. Plum and baked blackberries. This is rich, dark, monstrously concentrated yet open, expressive, articulate and very fine-grained in its tannins. There’s a lovely open-texture to the mid-palate accentuated by the considerable amplitude. Both sunny in disposition and fresh at the same time. Luminous yet opulent. This is perhaps the most impressive wine of the tasting, though it’s not my favourite. It’s most like the 2018 and, as that suggests, manages the vintage extremely well. 94. Chateau Léognan 2023 (Pessac-Léognan; 90% Cabernet Sauvignon; 10% Merlot; a final yield of 29 hl/ha, with mildew the great problem here, reducing considerably the volume of Merlot available for the final blend; pH 3.72; 12% alcohol). The highest proportion of Cabernet Sauvignon ever here, as the mildew clobbered the Merlot disproportionately. This is much more toasty aromatically and much less like an en primeur sample than the 2022. It’s rich, supple, the tannins breaking into the core of the less ample frame here and injecting a swirl of energy into the mid-palate. This is another colossal wine with a load of ultra fine-grained tannins still to resolve. It’s not perhaps quite as refined as the 2022 and is going through something of a slightly angular phase, but it just needs time. 93. Chateau Léognan 2024 (Pessac-Léognan; 75% Cabernet Sauvignon; 25% Merlot; a final yield of 35 hl/ha; pH 3.51; 12.9% alcohol; tasted from a barrel sample). Fresh, bright, lifted and distinctly crunchy in its dark berry and stone fruit signature. Creamy too, but with a lovely purity and freshness. Delicate in comparison to the 2018-2023 series, though still with plenty of stuffing for the vintage, this has been very nicely managed with the grip of the tannins imparting a lovely zippy freshness to the finish. Shapely, sapid and super for the vintage. 93+. Chateau Léognan 2025 (Pessac-Léognan; 60% Cabernet Sauvignon; 35% Merlot; 5% Petit Verdot for the first time; a final yield of 29 hl/ha; pH 3.55; 13.1% alcohol). Crunchy with lots of vertical lift, nicely expressively aromatically with stalky herbal notes alongside the plum, damson and bramble fruits, with a little wild sage and olive tapenade. Peony bulb, iris. Violet and rose. There’s a lovely pleasing purity to this and a nice sense of density and compactness too. But, above all this is luminous and crystalline, fluid and energetic. Vivid. Brilliant. One of the best. 92-94+.
